content: Dryer fabrics are tested for elongation, modulus, seam tensile strength, air permeability, air carrying capacity, heat transfer, caliper, friction coefficient, thermal stability and contamination tendency.
Hydrolysis resistance testing is performed in autoclaves or Parr Bomb tests under high temperature and pressure conditions to evaluate residual tensile strength after aging.
Dryer fabric contamination mainly consists of adhesives, wax, latex, resin and pitch deposits which reduce permeability and negatively affect sheet runnability.
Contamination decreases fabric permeability, reduces drying efficiency and may lead to sheet flutter, wrinkles, web breaks and productivity loss.
Anticontamination materials and dirt repellent coatings are used to reduce deposit build-up and improve dryer fabric cleanliness.
Dryer fabric cleaning systems include brushes, doctor blades, chemical cleaning and high pressure cleaning methods to maintain permeability and machine efficiency.
Fixed or rotary brushes are commonly used for cleaning monofilament dryer fabrics and removing contamination deposits from the paper side.
Doctor blades may be used continuously or intermittently to remove deposits from dryer fabrics, although high machine speeds can limit their application.
Chemical cleaning agents can effectively remove contamination, but polyester materials are sensitive to alkali and high temperatures, requiring careful rinsing and control.
Deep frozen CO2 cleaning is an advanced dryer fabric cleaning method capable of restoring permeability with minimal mechanical wear.
Conventional dryer sections use alternating top and bottom dryers with open draws that can create sheet flutter and sheet breaks at high machine speeds.
UnoRun dryer configuration uses a single fabric to support the sheet continuously through the dryer section, significantly reducing flutter and breaks.
UnoRun improves sheet runnability, reduces sheet breaks, improves edge quality and allows higher operating speeds by maintaining continuous sheet support.
UnoRun configurations may reduce drying capacity because the fabric separates the sheet from some dryer cylinders, reducing direct heat transfer.
UnoRun positions typically require smooth monofilament fabrics with uniform low permeability in the range of approximately 50 to 80 cfm for stable sheet adherence.
Single tier dryer sections require stable dryer fabrics capable of driving undriven cylinders while maintaining smooth nonmarking seam properties.
High permeability differences between seam area and fabric body can create sheet drying variations or welting marks on sensitive paper grades.
Neutral line represents the point within the dryer fabric thickness where speed difference is zero. Fabric construction influences paper stretching and shrinkage behavior.
Sheet flutter occurs mainly in unsupported open draws and can result in wrinkles, edge cracks and sheet breaks, especially at high machine speeds.
Short draw transfer systems reduce unsupported sheet length between dryer sections and significantly improve runnability and flutter control.
Pistol grip UnoRun transfer uses partial single-tier arrangement to improve sheet transfer stability while minimizing sheet flutter.
Dryer fabrics transport air within the dryer section. Excessive air carrying capacity may contribute to sheet instability and reduced drying efficiency.
Fabric contact area affects sheet support, marking tendency, heat transfer and drying efficiency. Surface weave design controls effective contact area.
Dryer fabric permeability must be selected according to machine design, paper grade, runnability requirements and drying conditions.
Spiral dryer fabrics are preferred in applications requiring excellent ventilation and dimensional stability but are less suitable for highly marking sensitive grades.
Hydrolysis resistant polyester monofilaments used in dryer fabrics are selected based on tenacity, elongation and thermal shrinkage properties for stable high temperature operation.
Spiral dryer fabrics use high shrinkage PET for spiral formation and low shrinkage PET for pintles to maintain dimensional stability during machine operation.
Dryer fabric finishing includes seam assist systems such as zip tape or Velcro, along with leader skirts and hook protectors to simplify installation and handling.
